BV Life

Designing an Internal Digital Workplace for Everyday Operations

◈ Product Designer
◉ Mobile App
◇ Internal Operations
◆ HR Tech
Scroll to explore
01 — Context & Problem

Building a
Daily Command
Center

BV Life is an internal company app designed to centralize everyday operational activities into a single platform.

The product included
Task management
Attendance & leave
Internal announcements
Lunch booking
Employee engagement

As the number of features continued to grow, the system gradually faced common enterprise product challenges:

⚡
Increasingly complex menus
⚡
Fragmented workflows
⚡
Notification overload
⚡
Difficulty finding the right actions quickly
⚡
Unclear approval flows
⚡
Valuable features becoming difficult to discover

In addition, different feature groups had very different usage frequencies. Attendance & task management were used daily, leave requests were used occasionally, and rewards & campaigns were more engagement-focused than utility-driven.

This turned the homepage and navigation system into one of the product's most critical UX challenges.

Flow 01 — Sign in & Home Screen
Sign In
Work Features
Life Features
02 — Target Users & Insights

Three Groups,
One Core
Friction

The product served three primary user groups. Although their goals differed, all three groups shared one common issue:

Core Insight
Too many repetitive actions
throughout the day.

During workflow analysis, key behavioral patterns emerged:

Users did not open the app to "view dashboards" — they simply wanted to complete tasks quickly.

Internal apps fail due to cognitive overload, not lack of features

These insights directly influenced how I approached navigation, homepage structure, and request flows.

👤
Employees
Daily task execution — check in, log time, submit requests, stay updated. Need fastest path to action.
👥
Team Leads
Approve requests, track team attendance, manage assignments. Need clear status visibility and quick approval flows.
🏢
HR / Admin
Configure policies, manage announcements, track engagement. Need oversight and communication tools.
03 — Product Hypothesis

Two Bets That
Guided Everything

Hypothesis 01 — Mental Models
If the product were reorganized around employees' mental models instead of internal departmental structures, users would complete tasks faster and experience lower cognitive load.
Hypothesis 02 — Command Center
If the homepage functioned as a "daily command center" rather than a dense overview dashboard, users would focus more easily on priority actions.

The core objective was helping users quickly answer: "What do I need to do right now?"

04 — Trade-offs

Not Everything
Could Be
Priority One

One of the biggest challenges was that not everything could be prioritized equally.

Key Trade-off Decisions
↓
Reduce the amount of information displayed on the homepage
↑
Prioritize quick actions over dense dashboards
→
Move engagement features into secondary layers
⊕
Standardize interaction patterns instead of creating custom UI for each module

The goal was to keep the experience clear and efficient for everyday operational tasks.

Check In / Check Out Flow
Check In
Check Out
Design Decision

Check in/out was the highest-frequency daily action. It needed to be one tap from the home screen — no navigation required.

05 — Solution Strategy

Designing an
Operational System

I approached the product as an operational system rather than a collection of isolated features. Key solution directions included reorganizing navigation around employee mental models, designing the homepage as a "daily command center", standardizing request flows, and building a notification hierarchy to reduce overload.

Flow 01 About Work — Leave Request
1
Empty State — First-time view shows empty leave list with clear CTA, guiding users to submit their first request.
2
Leave List — All past and pending requests displayed with status tags (pending/approved/rejected) for quick scanability.
3
Submit Leave Form — Streamlined form with leave type, date range, and reason. Pre-filled context reduces input friction.
4
Request Detail — Full visibility into approval status, timeline, and approver info after submission.
Design Principle

Standardized the request flow pattern for OT, leave, and WFH — reducing cognitive load through consistent mental models across all request types.

Empty State
Leave List
Submit Form
Request Detail
Flow 02 About Work — Log Time Request
Time Log List
Edit Timesheet
Request Detail
1
Time Log List — Overview of all logged time entries with daily/weekly breakdown and status indicators.
2
Edit Timesheet Form — Request to edit a timesheet entry with pre-filled values, project tag, and reason field.
3
Request Detail — Full audit trail of the edit request, including original vs. updated values and approval status.
Outcome

By reusing the same structural pattern across OT, leave, and time requests, users only needed to learn the flow once — then apply it everywhere.

06 — Information Architecture

Navigation Built
for Mental Models
Not Org Charts

Information Architecture became the main focus of the project. As the product expanded, the original structure evolved into a pattern of: new feature = new menu.

I reorganized the system based on user mental models rather than organizational structures.

Before
HR
Operations
Attendance
Internal News
Rewards
→
After
Work
Time
Community
Rewards
Profile
Impact of Restructuring
✓
Reduced feature fragmentation
✓
Improved discoverability
✓
Simplified navigation
✓
Enhanced daily usability

07 — UI & Design System

Friendly Enterprise
Design Direction

BV Life followed a "Friendly Enterprise" design direction: clear, professional, and approachable for employees. The UI system used card-based layouts, soft color hierarchy, modular sections, and reusable components.

Reusable Component Patterns
📋
Request Modules
Unified form pattern across all request types
⚡
Quick Actions
Home screen shortcuts for most-used flows
🔔
Notification Items
Tiered priority: action-required vs. informational
📊
Attendance Widgets
Always-visible status on the command center
08 — Metrics & Reflection

From Screen
Thinking to
System Thinking

BV Life was one of the first projects that helped me shift from "screen thinking" to "system thinking."

The project deepened my understanding of workflow design, information architecture, notification hierarchy, and balancing utility and engagement in enterprise products.

⚡
Request Completion
Time from open to submit — primary efficiency metric
✅
Approval Turnaround
Time for manager to act on pending requests
📣
Announcement Read Rate
% of employees opening internal news items
🧭
Navigation Efficiency
Depth of taps required to reach key actions
Key Learnings
01
Workflow design is more impactful than screen design
02
IA decisions shape the entire product experience
03
Notification hierarchy directly affects user trust
04
Balancing utility vs. engagement is a constant enterprise tension
Employee Engagement Feature
Campaign Leaderboard
Design Consideration

Engagement features like campaigns and leaderboards were intentionally placed in secondary navigation layers — surfaced contextually, not forced into daily workflows.
